title: Complete complex form vs single-page form vs multi-step form vs wizard
summary: Use a complete complex form when a product configuration has many related sections that must stay inspectable together, with section status, cross-section validation, draft preservation, and a review-ready save contract.
decisionRules: Prefer a complete complex form when related settings, policies, permissions, or enterprise configuration fields need one inspectable editing surface rather than a short one-page submit or a sequential transaction.
Prefer a single-page form when the field set is compact, related, low branching, and can be reviewed comfortably before one submit without section navigation.

Prefer a multi-step form when users are completing a long transaction across saved pages and each page has a clear answer-collection role, especially for applications, checkout, compliance, or public-service journeys.
Prefer a wizard when the product must guide a setup, import, or creation task through dependent steps with a final finish action, step-specific tests, generated previews, or cancel semantics.

A complete complex form should expose section names, section status, current section, validation errors, stale review state, and save readiness without making users infer progress from scroll position.

Keep related controls grouped with headings, fieldsets, legends, persistent labels, helper text, and aligned validation messages; do not compress the form into a spreadsheet-like grid of abbreviations.
Use cross-section validation when one section changes the requirement or meaning of another section, and preserve all entered values when moving to an error-owning section.
Provide an error summary that points to the affected section or field group, then show the field-level error beside the owning input.
Mark required or optional fields according to the dominant pattern in that product form; for long product configuration forms, required labels are often clearer when most fields are optional.
Do not use a complete complex form when research shows users need one thing per page, heavy branching, mobile-first recovery, or per-answer autosave; use multi-step or single-question pages instead.
Use progressive disclosure inside a section only for secondary settings; do not hide required fields, dependencies, section status, or save-blocking errors behind collapsed advanced panels.
Before save, offer review or summary behavior for high-risk settings so users can inspect dependencies and section status without leaving the form.
failureModes: A dense grid of unrelated settings is called a complex form but has no section ownership, labels, validation recovery, or review state.
The product splits tightly related settings across pages, so users cannot compare a threshold in one section with the approval rule it changes.
A wizard is used for ordinary settings editing, adding blocked future steps and cancel behavior when users only needed a sectioned configuration surface.
A long public-service form is kept on one page even though one-question pages, branching, and saved answers would reduce errors.
Cross-section dependencies fail silently, letting users save a policy where one section contradicts another.
Error messages appear only at the top of the form, forcing users to search through many sections without field-level ownership.

sources.note: Carbon form guidance distinguishes longer product-oriented complex forms from simple forms and documents layout, labels, required indicators, helper text, and validation states.
USWDS form-template guidance supports DOM order matching visual order, fieldsets, legends, required indicators, vertical layout, and validation aligned with inputs.
GOV.UK form-structure guidance provides the counterweight: start with one thing per page and only merge fields when research and task context support it.
